sources.title: Cambridge OCR Level 3 FSMQ Additional Mathematics 6993 specification

statements.statementText: It is expected that calculators available in the assessment will include an iterative function, such as an ANS key.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 9, line IDs p9-r3-b0-l0,p9-r3-b0-l1,p9-r3-b0-l2, source-p9-r3-b0

statements.statementText: Allowable calculators can be used for any function they can perform.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 9, line IDs p9-r3-b1-l0,p9-r3-b1-l1, source-p9-r3-b1

statements.statementText: 1. Candidates are advised to write down explicitly any expressions, including integrals, that they use the calculator to evaluate.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 9, line IDs p9-r3-b3-l0,p9-r3-b3-l1,p9-r3-b3-l2, source-p9-r3-b3

statements.statementText: 2. Candidates are advised to write down the values of any parameters and variables that they input into the calculator. Candidates are not expected to write down data transferred from question paper to calculator.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 9, line IDs p9-r3-b4-l0,p9-r3-b4-l1,p9-r3-b4-l2,p9-r3-b4-l3, source-p9-r3-b4

statements.statementText: 3. Correct mathematical notation (rather than ‘calculator notation’) should be used. Incorrect notation may result in loss of marks.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 9, line IDs p9-r3-b5-l0,p9-r3-b5-l1,p9-r3-b5-l2, source-p9-r3-b5

statements.statementText: Exact An exact answer is one where numbers are not given in rounded form. The answer will often contain an irrational number such as √3 or π and these numbers should be given in that form when an exact answer is required. The use of the word ‘exact’ also tells learners that rigorous (exact) working is expected in the answer to the question. e.g. Find the exact solution of 3x = 2 . The correct answer is x = 2/3 or x = 0.6̇ , not x = 0.67 or similar.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r5-b0-l0,p10-r5-b0-l1,p10-r5-b0-l10,p10-r5-b0-l11,p10-r5-b0-l2,p10-r5-b0-l3,p10-r5-b0-l4,p10-r5-b0-l5,p10-r5-b0-l6,p10-r5-b0-l7,p10-r5-b0-l8,p10-r5-b0-l9, source-p10-r5-b0

statements.statementText: Know and use algebraic vocabulary and notation.
notesText: i.e. constant, coefficient, expression, equation, identity,

reviewNotes: Covers the symbolic conventions and notation.
Function notation is mentioned in the notes.
Distinction between equation and identity is part of vocabulary.
Generalised numbers covers the concept of variables and unknowns.
Confirmed by the two-pass concept-accounting review and Codex final verification.

statements.statementText: AL10 Understand and use notation of recurrence relationships to describe and determine sequences.
examplesText: e.g. xₙ₊₁ = xₙ + a
e.g. xₙ₊₁ = axₙ
e.g. xₙ₊₂ = xₙ + xₙ₊₁

reviewNotes: Recurrence relationships are term-to-term rules.

examplesText: e.g. modelling compound interest.

statements.statementText: AL2 Simplify expressions involving algebraic fractions and square roots.
examplesText: e.g. Simplify 2/(x − 1) − 1/(x + 1).

Surd simplification is exemplified by the second set of examples.
Rationalizing the denominator is required in the example.

statements.statementText: Find linear factors of a polynomial.
notesText: Includes the use of the factor theorem.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 12, line IDs p12-r22-b0-l0,p12-r23-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-AL4

reviewNotes: Factor theorem is the standard method for finding linear factors.

statements.statementText: AL5 Complete the square of a quadratic polynomial.
examplesText: ax² + bx + c ≡ a(x + p)² + q
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 12, line IDs p12-r25-b0-l0,p12-r26-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-AL5

reviewNotes: Completing the square is a specific technique for rewriting quadratics.

statements.statementText: Set up and solve problems leading to linear, quadratic and cubic equations in one unknown, and to simultaneous equations in two unknowns.
notesText: Problems could be set in mathematical or

reviewNotes: Solving linear equations in one unknown is directly required.
Covers cubic polynomials including solving cubic equations.
Constructing linear equations from contexts is part of setting up problems.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Set up and solve problems leading to
reviewNotes: Solving unstructured problems requires selecting and applying appropriate methods.

statements.statementText: Manipulate inequalities.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 12, line IDs p12-r33-b0-l0,p12-r34-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-AL7

reviewNotes: Manipulating linear inequalities includes solving and rearranging; the example is a linear inequality.

statements.statementText: AL8 Set up and solve linear and quadratic inequalities algebraically and graphically.
examplesText: e.g. solve −3 < 2x − 1 < 5
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 12, line IDs p12-r36-b0-l0,p12-r36-b0-l1,p12-r37-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-AL8

reviewNotes: Solving linear inequalities algebraically and graphically is a required skill.
Solving quadratic inequalities algebraically and graphically is included.
Graphical solution may involve intersections of graphs.

statements.statementText: Illustrate linear inequalities in two variables.
notesText: i.e. the use of appropriate shading.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 12, line IDs p12-r39-b0-l0,p12-r40-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-AL9

reviewNotes: Illustrating linear inequalities with shading directly corresponds to regions defined by inequalities.

statements.statementText: EN1 Understand and be able to apply the binomial expansion of (a + b)ⁿ where n is a positive integer.
examplesText: e.g. Expand (2 + 3x)⁵ in ascending powers of x.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 13, line IDs p13-r5-b0-l0,p13-r5-b0-l1,p13-r6-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-EN1

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: binomial expansion of (a + b)ⁿ where n is a positive integer
reviewNotes: Direct mapping to binomial expansion for positive integer powers.
Binomial coefficients are a fundamental component of the expansion.

statements.statementText: EN2 Construct and use tree diagrams, two way tables, Venn Diagrams or the binomial distribution to enumerate outcomes.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 13, line IDs p13-r8-b0-l0,p13-r8-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-EN2

reviewNotes: Tree diagrams are explicitly listed as a method for enumerating outcomes.
Two-way tables are explicitly listed.
Venn Diagrams are explicitly listed.
Binomial distribution enumeration uses combinations to count outcomes.
The statement explicitly requires systematic listing of outcomes, which matches the definition of systematic listing.
Enumerating outcomes constructs the complete set of all possible outcomes, i.e., the sample space.

statements.statementText: EN3 Use the product rule for counting numbers of outcomes of combined events.
examplesText: e.g. Number of outcomes rolling n dice is 6ⁿ.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 13, line IDs p13-r10-b0-l0,p13-r10-b0-l1,p13-r11-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-EN3

reviewNotes: The product rule for counting is exactly the multiplication principle node.

statements.statementText: EN4 Enumerate the number of ways of obtaining an ordered linear subset (permutation) of r elements from a set of n distinct objects.
notesText: Includes the use of the notation ₙPᵣ (ⁿPᵣ).
examplesText: e.g. How many ways of awarding two prizes in a group of ten people.
e.g. Number of arrangements of n distinct objects is n! .
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 13, line IDs p13-r12-b0-l0,p13-r12-b0-l1,p13-r13-b0-l0,p13-r13-b0-l1,p13-r16-b0-l0,p13-r17-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-EN4

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: ordered linear subset (permutation) of r elements from a set of n distinct objects
reviewNotes: Direct mapping to permutations counting.

statements.statementText: EN5 Enumerate an unordered subset (combination) of r elements from a set of n distinct objects.
notesText: Includes the use of the notation ₙCᵣ (ⁿCᵣ).
examplesText: e.g. How many ways are there of choosing two people out of a group of ten to sit on a committee?
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 13, line IDs p13-r18-b0-l0,p13-r18-b0-l1,p13-r19-b0-l0,p13-r19-b0-l1,p13-r19-b1-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-EN5

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: unordered subset (combination) of r elements from a set of n distinct objects
reviewNotes: Direct mapping to combinations counting.

statements.statementText: EN6 Solve problems about outcomes, including problems in the context of probability.
examplesText: e.g. Find the probability of obtaining at least two sixes when five dice are rolled.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 13, line IDs p13-r21-b0-l0,p13-r21-b0-l1,p13-r22-b0-l0,p13-r22-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-EN6

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Find the probability of obtaining at least two sixes when five dice are rolled
reviewNotes: Equally likely outcomes form the basis for probability calculations in this example.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Find the probability of obtaining at least two sixes when five dice are rolled
reviewNotes: Combinations are used to count the number of ways to obtain a specific number of sixes.

statements.statementText: CG1 Calculate the distance between two points.
examplesText: e.g. √((x₂ − x₁)² + (y₂ − y₁)²).

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Find the mid-point of a line segment
reviewNotes: Direct mapping to the midpoint formula.

statements.statementText: CG3 Know and use the equation of a circle (x − a)² + (y − b)² = r², where (a, b) is the centre and r is the radius of the circle.
examplesText: e.g. A(1, 1) and B(5, 7) are the ends of a diameter of the circle. Show that the equation of the circle is (x − 3)² + (y − 4)² = 13.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 14, line IDs p14-r13-b0-l6,p14-r13-b0-l7,p14-r13-b0-l8,p14-r8-b0-l0,p14-r8-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-CG3

reviewNotes: Direct statement of the centre-radius form.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: A(1, 1) and B(5, 7) are the ends of a diameter of the circle. Show that the equation of the circle is (x − 3)² + (y − 4)² = 13.
reviewNotes: Example requires finding the centre via midpoint.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: A(1, 1) and B(5, 7) are the ends of a diameter of the circle. Show that the equation of the circle is (x − 3)² + (y − 4)² = 13.
reviewNotes: Example requires finding the radius via distance.

statements.statementText: CG4 Sketch and plot linear, polynomial, trigonometric and exponential functions.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 14, line IDs p14-r10-b0-l0,p14-r10-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-CG4

reviewNotes: Linear graphs are a direct match.
Quadratic graphs are a subset of polynomial graphs.
Cubic graphs are a subset of polynomial graphs.
Quartic graphs are a subset of polynomial graphs.
Sine graph is a subset of trigonometric graphs.
Cosine graph is a subset of trigonometric graphs.
Tangent graph is a subset of trigonometric graphs.
Exponential graphs match the exponential function with base a.

statements.statementText: CG5 Know, understand and use gradient, intercept, tangent and normal in problems involving points that can be defined by equations and inequalities.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 14, line IDs p14-r14-b0-l0,p14-r14-b0-l1,p14-r14-b0-l2, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-CG5

reviewNotes: Gradient of a straight line.
The y = mx + c form gives the intercept.
Tangents and normals using derivatives.

statements.statementText: CG6 Express real situations in terms of linear inequalities.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Express real situations in terms of linear inequalities

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Express real situations in terms of linear inequalities

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: CG6 Express real situations in terms of linear inequalities.
reviewNotes: The statement explicitly requires forming linear inequalities from real situations, which covers the formation aspect of the node; however, the node's broader scope includes manipulation with sign-reversal and expressing solution sets, which are not explicitly assessed here.

statements.statementText: CG7 Use graphs of linear inequalities to solve 2-dimensional maximisation and minimisation problems.
examplesText: e.g. Given 4x + 7y < 56, 6x + 3y < 54, x > 0, y > 0 find the maximum value of x + y.

statements.statementText: CA1 Differentiate kxⁿ where n is a positive integer or 0, and the sum of such functions.
notesText: i.e. use of notation dy/dx, f′(x), ẋ.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r5-b0-l0,p16-r5-b0-l1,p16-r6-b0-l0,p16-r8-b0-l0,p16-r8-b0-l1,p16-r8-b0-l2, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-CA1

reviewNotes: Differentiates power functions using the power rule.
Differentiates sums and constant multiples of functions.
Notes explicitly list derivative notations (Leibniz, prime, dot). CALC-DIFF-CONC-NOTN is the specific leaf for derivative notation, more precise than the current ALGF-EXP-NOTN-SYMB link.

statements.statementText: CA10 Know what is meant by an indefinite and a definite integral.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r17-b0-l0,p16-r18-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-CA10

reviewNotes: Note explicitly addresses the constant of integration, which is part of indefinite integrals.
Statement includes knowing what a definite integral is.

statements.statementText: CA11 Evaluate definite integrals.
examplesText: ∫₀¹ x³ + 4x² - 1 dx.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p10-r7-b0-l0,p16-r19-b0-l0,p16-r20-b0-l0,p16-r23-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-CA11

reviewNotes: Core concept of definite integrals.
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus is used to evaluate definite integrals.
The example requires integrating powers of x using the power rule for integration, which is a leaf concept not yet linked.
The example involves a sum of terms, so integrating it requires linearity (constant multiple and sum/difference rules). The missing leaf CALC-INTE-RULE-V5A76A4288 captures this explicitly.

statements.statementText: CA12 Find the area between a curve, two ordinates and the x-axis.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r21-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-CA12

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: area between a curve, two ordinates and the x-axis
reviewNotes: Calculates area under a curve using definite integrals.

statements.statementText: CA13 Find the area between two curves.
examplesText: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx − ∫ₐᵇ g(x) dx
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r21-b1-l0,p16-r24-b0-l0,p16-r25-b0-l0,p16-r26-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-CA13

reviewNotes: Calculates area between two curves by subtracting integrals.
The example demonstrates using definite integrals to compute area.

statements.statementText: CA14 Use differentiation and integration with respect to time to solve simple problems involving variable acceleration.

Using integration of acceleration to find velocity.
Using integration of velocity to find displacement.

statements.statementText: CA4 Find the equation of a tangent and normal at any point on a curve.
examplesText: e.g. Find the equations of normal to the curve y = x³ − 2x + 3 at the point (1, 2).

reviewNotes: Finds equations of tangents and normals to curves.

statements.statementText: CA8 Integrate kxⁿ where n is a positive integer or 0, and the sum of such functions.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Integrate kxⁿ where n is a positive integer or 0, and the sum of such functions.
reviewNotes: Direct statement of integrating power functions.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: and the sum of such functions
reviewNotes: Statement requires integrating sums of functions, which invokes the linearity (sum) rule for integration. Relation adjusted to 'partial' because the statement is a compound requirement.

statements.statementText: CA9 Be aware that integration is the reverse of differentiation.
examplesText: e.g. be able to find the equation of a curve, given its gradient function and one point.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Be aware that integration is the reverse of differentiation.
reviewNotes: Core concept of integration as reverse differentiation.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: be able to find the equation of a curve, given its gradient function and one point.
reviewNotes: Example involves using the constant of integration.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: be able to find the equation of a curve, given its gradient function and one point.

statements.statementText: NM1 Solve equations approximately by considering the change of sign.

reviewNotes: Statement matches an iterative formula for root finding.

statements.statementText: NM3 Recognise when these numerical methods may fail.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 17, line IDs p17-r5-b2-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-NM3

reviewNotes: Directly covers recognising failure of sign change method.
Understanding convergence is essential to recognise failure of iterative methods.

statements.statementText: NM8 Recognise whether an estimate would be an over or under estimate, and understand how to calculate an improved estimate.

reviewNotes: Exact match for linearising exponential and power-law relationships.

statements.statementText: EL5 Estimate values of k and a or k and n from graphs.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 18, line IDs p18-r18-b0-l0,p18-r19-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-EL5

reviewNotes: Estimating parameters from linearised graphs is part of the linearising node.

statements.statementText: EL6 Solve equations of the form aˣ = b for a > 0.
examplesText: Use logarithms to solve the equation 3²ˣ⁺¹ = 4¹⁰⁰, giving your answer correct to 3 significant figures. The answer is x = 62.6, but the learner must include the steps log 3²ˣ⁺¹ = log 4¹⁰⁰, (2x+1) log 3 = log 4¹⁰⁰ and an intermediate evaluation step, e.g. 2x+1 = 126.18…. Using the solve function on a calculator to skip one of these steps would not result in a complete analytical method.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 18, line IDs p10-r6-b0-l0,p10-r6-b0-l1,p10-r6-b0-l2,p10-r6-b0-l3,p10-r6-b0-l4,p10-r6-b0-l5,p10-r6-b0-l6,p10-r6-b0-l7,p18-r20-b0-l0,p18-r21-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-OCR-6993-EL6
